cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Okay, here is one that trips up more candidates than you would think.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: You write a REST Assured test with given dot auth dot basic, you pass in a username and password that you know are correct, because they work in Postman, and the API still comes back with a 401.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: The interviewer leans in and asks why the exact same credentials fail in your code but pass in the browser.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: What do you say?

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: And if your honest answer is that you would just add a wait and re-run it, this episode is for you.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: I'm Leo, I write automation for a living and I still remember blanking on this exact question.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And I'm Maya, I've spent years as a staff SDET and I used to run interview loops, so I've watched that question sort candidates into two piles in about 30 seconds.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Today we're doing REST Assured, the Java library for API testing, and we'll come back and resolve that 401 mystery in the trap corner, because it has a beautiful one-line answer.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: So before we get into syntax, why does this topic carry so much weight in SDET interviews, because it feels like every job posting lists it.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Because API tests are where most teams get their real regression coverage, they're faster and far more stable than UI tests, and REST Assured is the de facto standard in the Java world.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: So when an interviewer asks about it, they're really asking whether you can build a maintainable test framework, not just whether you memorized method names.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: The definition you should have ready is that REST Assured is an open source Java DSL, a domain specific language, for testing REST APIs, and its whole value is that it removes the boilerplate you'd write with raw HttpClient, giving you a fluent given when then syntax, built in JSON and XML parsing, and Hamcrest matchers for assertions.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Okay, so let's start with that given when then structure, because it's the first thing anyone sees.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Right, and here's the analogy I always give, sending a request with REST Assured is like shipping a package at the post office.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: The given block is everything you do at the counter before anything ships, you pack the box, you write the address, you tape on the labels, which in code means setting the base URI, the headers, the content type, the query params, and the request body.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: The when block is the moment you hand the package over, that's the HTTP verb, get, post, put, or delete.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And the then block is the delivery confirmation, where you check that it arrived, so you assert the status code, the headers, and the body.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: So given is arrange, when is act, then is assert, it's literally the triple A pattern wearing a BDD costume.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: And in code that reads like, given, dot baseUri, dot header, dot queryParam, then dot when, dot get on slash users, then dot then, dot statusCode 200, dot body with a matcher.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Exactly, and here's a small flex interviewers love, the given block is actually optional sugar, for a bare GET you can just write when dot get and then chain your assertions, and knowing that shows you understand the structure rather than just copying it.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: While we're at the counter, know your labels, pathParam substitutes into a placeholder in the URL, like users slash curly braces userId, queryParam appends things like question mark active equals true, and formParam is for form encoded bodies.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And for a POST body you have options, you can pass a raw JSON string, which is ugly and brittle, you can pass a Java Map and REST Assured serializes it for you, or, and this is the mature answer, you can pass a POJO.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Hold that POJO thought, because I want to get to assertions first.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Once the response comes back, how do I actually check what's inside the box?

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: This is chunk 2, and the analogy here is a street address.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: A JSON response is like an apartment building, and a JsonPath expression is the address that gets you to one specific apartment, so data dot email walks into the data object and pulls out the email field, and data bracket 0 dot id gets the id of the first element in the array.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: The body method takes 2 things, that address, and a Hamcrest matcher that says what should live there, equalTo, greaterThan, notNullValue, containsString, hasKey, all statically imported from Hamcrest.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: And for whole arrays, I remember there are some almost magical expressions, like calling size directly in the path.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Yes, because under the hood REST Assured uses Groovy's GPath, which means your address can contain logic, you can write data dot size in parentheses and assert it equals 6, you can write data dot id and get back the whole list of ids to check with hasItems, and you can even write data dot findAll with a condition like it dot id greater than 8, then take the size of that filtered list.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Mentioning that it's Groovy GPath, with findAll, find, and collect available, is a nice depth signal.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Wait, so if I can check every value by address, why would I ever need JSON schema validation, isn't that redundant?

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Great question, and the answer stays inside the building analogy, value assertions check the furniture in one apartment, schema validation checks the blueprint of the whole building.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Values change from environment to environment, but the contract, the field names, the types, which fields are required, that should be stable, so you add the json schema validator module and assert body matchesJsonSchemaInClasspath against a schema file.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: One line guards the entire structure, and that's more robust than pinning 20 individual values.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Okay, now the POJO thing.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: My memory hook is that serialization is object to JSON on the way out, and deserialization is JSON to object on the way in.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: That's exactly it, you define a plain Java class with fields, getters and setters, pass the object to dot body and REST Assured serializes it into the JSON request, and on the response side you call extract dot as with the class, and get a typed Java object back to assert on.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: It delegates the actual conversion to Jackson or Gson, whichever is on the classpath, it auto detects.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And when they ask why POJOs beat raw strings, say type safety, refactor friendliness, and reuse, because a hand written JSON string with escaped quotes breaks silently the moment the API changes.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Alright, chunk 3, because so far every example is one self contained test, and real frameworks have hundreds.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: How do I stop repeating the same base URI and headers everywhere?

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: With specifications, and the analogy here is company letterhead.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Instead of handwriting your company name, logo, and address on every single letter, you print stationery once and every letter starts from it.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: A RequestSpecification, built with RequestSpecBuilder, is that letterhead for requests, base URI, content type, auth header, defined once and applied with dot spec.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And there's a matching ResponseSpecification for the assertions you make everywhere, expect status 200, expect JSON content type, expect response time under 2000 milliseconds, so each test only asserts what's unique to it.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: And I'm guessing this connects to how you'd structure the whole framework, because interviewers love the architecture question.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: They do, and there's a key phrase they're listening for, a service layer, sometimes called an endpoint layer.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: It's the API equivalent of the Page Object Model from UI automation, you wrap the raw HTTP calls into reusable methods like createUser or getUserById, so your tests contain business logic and assertions, not HTTP plumbing.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Around that you'd describe a base test class for common setup, a models package for POJOs, utils for config readers and token managers, resources for schemas and test data and the testng xml, and Allure or Extent for reporting, with Maven running it all through the surefire plugin via mvn clean test, and profiles to switch environments.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Which brings us to authentication, because tokens are usually part of that setup story.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Right, and the realistic flow is like a wristband at a festival, you show your ticket once at the box office, they give you a wristband, and after that you just flash the wristband at every stage.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: In code, you post credentials to the login endpoint, extract dot path pulls the token out of the response, and then every protected call uses auth dot oauth2 with that token, or equivalently a header of Authorization Bearer plus the token.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: In a framework you wrap that in a getAuthToken method, cache it, and bake it into your request spec.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And know the menu, REST Assured supports basic, digest, OAuth 1, OAuth 2, and plain API key headers.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Okay, trap corner time, and I believe you owe us a resolution to that cold open 401.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: I do, so here's the mechanics.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Plain dot basic is challenged authentication, meaning the client first sends the request with no credentials at all, waits for the server to respond with a 401 and a WWW dash Authenticate challenge, and only then resends with the header, so 2 round trips.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: The trap is that many modern APIs never send that proper challenge, they just reject you, so your credentials never get sent at all, and the test fails even though the password is right.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: The fix is one word, preemptive, you write auth dot preemptive dot basic, and the Authorization header goes out on the very first request.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Like walking up to a club and showing your ID before the bouncer asks, instead of waiting for a question that never comes.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: So my memory hook is, challenged waits to be asked, preemptive shows ID at the door, and if basic auth mysteriously 401s with correct credentials, say the word preemptive.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And there's a second classic trap while we're here, static configuration.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Setting RestAssured dot baseURI is convenient, but it's global mutable state, like a shared office whiteboard, and the moment you run TestNG with parallel equals methods and a thread count, threads start overwriting each other's writing and tests get flaky for no visible reason.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: The senior answer is local request specifications per test, or ThreadLocal for anything shared like tokens, and calling RestAssured dot reset in your after hooks if you did touch the static config.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: So, in the room, how does this topic actually surface in an interview?

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Three ways, usually in sequence.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: First conceptual, define REST Assured, explain given when then, compare preemptive and challenged auth.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Then live coding, and the classic ask is a full CRUD flow, create a user with POST expecting 201, extract the id from the response, GET it back expecting 200, PUT an update, DELETE it expecting 204, and the skill being tested is chaining, extract dot path from response 1 feeding request 2.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And third, rapid fire HTTP fundamentals, they will ask PUT versus PATCH, and you say PUT replaces the entire resource while PATCH applies a partial update, and they will ask which methods are idempotent, and you say GET, PUT, DELETE, and HEAD, but not POST, because idempotent means repeating the request leaves the server in the same state.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Also worth having the TestNG lifecycle cold, BeforeSuite, then BeforeTest, then BeforeClass, then BeforeMethod, then the Test itself, and the After hooks unwind in reverse, my hook is that the rings close from the outside in and open from the inside out.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Nicely put.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Alright, 60 second recap, 3 takeaways.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: If they ask what REST Assured is, say a Java DSL for REST API testing with fluent given when then syntax, built in JsonPath via Groovy GPath, Hamcrest matchers, and Jackson based POJO serialization, then land the post office picture, pack in given, ship in when, confirm in then.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: If they ask about basic auth behaving strangely, say challenged auth waits for a 401 challenge that many APIs never send, so use preemptive to put the header on the first request, one round trip, no silent failure.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And if they ask how you'd build the framework, say request and response specifications as reusable letterhead, a service layer so tests hold logic instead of plumbing, POJOs over raw strings, schema validation to guard the contract, and no static state so parallel runs stay green.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Say those 3 things well and you've covered most of what a REST Assured round can throw at you.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: My brain feels properly serialized.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Next time you have 10 minutes, come back for the deeper HTTP episode, because idempotency and status codes deserve their own hour.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Keep practicing that CRUD flow until you can type it half asleep, and we'll see you in the next one.
